column4: workflow
column5: resolution
column12: annotation

column4: Phase 1 - Alert Verification: Acknowledge alert in monitoring dashboard and verify pod status via kubectl get pods; Phase 2 - Diagnosis: Inspect pod logs using kubectl logs --previous to identify crash reason; Phase 3 - Remediation: Check resource limits and adjust if OOMKilled; Phase 4 - Validation: Monitor pod stability for 15 minutes; Phase 5 - Closure: Document root cause in incident ticket.
column5: Adjusted resource limits and restarted pod. Verified stability via monitoring dashboard. No further crashes observed within 15 minute window. Incident closed after stakeholder notification.
column12: Requires Platform Engineering escalation if crash persists after resource adjustment. SLA breach risk high. Mandatory post-incident review within 24 hours. Update runbook K8S-001 with specific OOM patterns observed.

column4: Step 1 - Triage: Confirm lag metrics via Grafana dashboard and identify affected topic partitions; Step 2 - Investigation: Check consumer group status and identify slow consumers; Step 3 - Action: Scale consumer instances horizontally to handle backlog; Step 4 - Verification: Monitor lag reduction rate and ensure throughput matches production rate; Step 5 - Follow-up: Review consumer logic for bottlenecks if lag persists.
column5: Scaled consumer group from 5 to 10 instances. Lag reduced to zero within 20 minutes. Verified message processing rate matches producer rate. Incident resolved after confirming no data loss.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if scaling does not reduce lag within 30 minutes. Check for poison messages in dead letter queue. Update capacity planning model based on this spike.

column4: Procedure 1 - Identify: Check API Gateway logs for 429 responses and identify offending tenant ID; Procedure 2 - Contact: Notify tenant account manager regarding limit breach; Procedure 3 - Mitigate: Temporarily increase rate limit quota if business critical; Procedure 4 - Monitor: Watch traffic patterns for abuse vs legitimate spike; Procedure 5 - Document: Log quota change in billing system for reconciliation.
column5: Temporarily increased rate limit quota by 50% for critical tenant. Notified account manager to discuss permanent plan upgrade. Monitored traffic for 1 hour to ensure stability. Quota change logged in billing system.
column12: Security team must approve quota increases exceeding 100%. Ensure tenant is not exhibiting DDoS behavior. Review billing tier eligibility after incident closure.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Review CloudTrail logs for unauthorized access attempts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Compare current bucket policy against security baseline template; Phase 3 - Correction: Revert policy to private access unless public access is explicitly required; Phase 4 - Validation: Run security scanner to confirm no public exposure; Phase 5 - Notification: Inform security team of configuration drift.
column5: Reverted bucket policy to private access. Validated via security scanner that no public access exists. Notified security team for audit log review. Configuration drift documented in change management system.
column12: Immediate escalation to Security Lead required. Potential data breach risk. Initiate incident response protocol if unauthorized access confirmed. Mandatory security review within 4 hours.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge rotation failure alert from secrets manager; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check rotation lambda logs for permission errors or API failures; Step 3 - Fix: Update IAM policies to grant required rotation permissions; Step 4 - Retry: Manually trigger rotation process and verify success; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm applications can retrieve new secret version without interruption.
column5: Updated IAM policy to allow secretsmanager:PutSecretValue. Manually triggered rotation successfully. Verified application connectivity using new secret version. No service interruption reported.
column12: Escalate to Security Engineering if rotation fails twice. Ensure all dependent services are updated with new secret version. Audit IAM policies for least privilege compliance.

column4: Phase 1 - Assessment: Check Logstash/Elasticsearch queue depth and ingestion rates; Phase 2 - Resource Check: Verify CPU and memory usage on aggregator nodes; Phase 3 - Scaling: Add additional aggregator nodes to distribute load; Phase 4 - Optimization: Adjust batch sizes and flush intervals for better throughput; Phase 5 - Monitoring: Confirm backlog clearance and stable ingestion rates.
column5: Added 2 aggregator nodes to cluster. Adjusted batch size to 5000 events. Backlog cleared within 45 minutes. Ingestion rates stabilized at normal levels. Monitoring confirmed no data loss.
column12: Contact Observability Team if backlog exceeds 1 hour worth of data. Review log retention policies to manage storage costs. Update alerting thresholds based on new baseline capacity.

column3: ETL job failure due to schema change
column4: Step 1 - Identify: Check orchestration dashboard for failed job status and error logs; Step 2 - Analyze: Compare source schema with expected schema to identify mismatches; Step 3 - Remediate: Update transformation logic to handle new schema fields; Step 4 - Replay: Re-run failed job from last successful checkpoint; Step 5 - Validate: Verify data integrity in target warehouse tables.
column5: Updated transformation logic to map new source fields. Replayed job from checkpoint successfully. Verified row counts and checksums in target warehouse. Data integrity confirmed via automated tests.
column12: Notify Data Product Owner of schema change impact. Implement schema evolution strategy to prevent future failures. Update data dictionary with new field definitions.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Run configuration compliance scan against git repository baseline; Phase 2 - Identification: List specific parameters that differ from baseline; Phase 3 - Correction: Push correct configuration from repository to server; Phase 4 - Verification: Re-run compliance scan to confirm drift resolution; Phase 5 - Audit: Investigate cause of manual change to prevent recurrence.
column5: Pushed correct configuration from git repository. Compliance scan passed with 100% match. Investigated change log and identified manual override by engineer. Reinforced change management policy.
column12: Escalate to Platform Lead if drift recurs within 24 hours. Ensure all changes go through pull request process. Update automation to prevent manual overrides.

column4: Step 1 - Monitor: Check service mesh telemetry for increased proxy latency; Step 2 - Trace: Use distributed tracing to identify slow service hops; Step 3 - Tune: Adjust proxy resource limits and connection pool settings; Step 4 - Restart: Rolling restart of sidecar proxies to apply changes; Step 5 - Validate: Confirm latency returns to baseline levels via dashboard.
column5: Adjusted sidecar CPU limits and connection pool size. Performed rolling restart of proxies. Latency returned to baseline within 10 minutes. Tracing confirmed no bottlenecks.
column12: Engage Network Team if latency persists after tuning. Review mesh configuration for inefficient routing rules. Update performance baseline documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Analysis: Review CDN analytics for cache hit ratio trends and miss patterns; Phase 2 - Investigation: Check origin server health and TTL configurations; Phase 3 - Optimization: Increase TTL for static assets and purge stale content; Phase 4 - Verification: Monitor hit ratio improvement over next hour; Phase 5 - Documentation: Record configuration changes in CDN management portal.
column5: Increased TTL for static assets to 7 days. Purged stale content from edge nodes. Cache hit ratio improved from 60% to 90%. Origin load reduced significantly. Changes documented in portal.
column12: Contact CDN Provider if hit ratio does not improve. Review content versioning strategy to avoid unnecessary purges. Optimize origin shield configuration.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge node not ready alert from cluster monitoring; Step 2 - Diagnose: SSH into node and check kubelet service status and logs; Step 3 - Remediate: Restart kubelet service and check network connectivity to control plane; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm node returns to Ready state in kubectl get nodes; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for recurring readiness issues over 24 hours.
column5: Restarted kubelet service and fixed CNI plugin configuration. Node returned to Ready state within 5 minutes. Verified pod scheduling on node. Monitoring confirmed stability.
column12: Escalate to Infrastructure Team if node fails again. Check underlying VM health and hypervisor metrics. Update node maintenance runbook with troubleshooting steps.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify offline partitions via broker monitoring dashboard; Phase 2 - Investigation: Check broker logs for disk errors or leadership election failures; Phase 3 - Recovery: Trigger preferred leader election to restore partitions; Phase 4 - Validation: Verify producer and consumer connectivity to partition; Phase 5 - Closure: Document partition recovery in incident log.
column5: Triggered preferred leader election successfully. Partitions came online within 2 minutes. Verified producer writes and consumer reads. No data loss confirmed. Incident logged.
column12: Immediate escalation to Messaging Platform Team. Check disk health on broker hosting partition. Review replication factor settings for high availability.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge certificate expiry warning alert; Step 2 - Verify: Check certificate validity dates and domain coverage; Step 3 - Renew: Generate new CSR and obtain certificate from CA; Step 4 - Deploy: Upload new certificate to API Gateway and associate with listeners; Step 5 - Test: Verify HTTPS connectivity using new certificate without errors.
column5: Renewed certificate via automated CA integration. Deployed to API Gateway listeners. Tested HTTPS connectivity successfully. No downtime during deployment. Alert cleared.
column12: Escalate to Security Team if renewal fails. Implement automated certificate rotation to prevent future expiry. Update certificate inventory database.

column4: Phase 1 - Triage: Identify failed multipart uploads via storage logs; Phase 2 - Diagnosis: Check network stability and client-side upload logs; Phase 3 - Cleanup: Abort incomplete multipart uploads to free storage; Phase 4 - Retry: Instruct client to retry upload with exponential backoff; Phase 5 - Monitor: Verify successful completion of retried uploads.
column5: Aborted incomplete uploads to reclaim storage space. Client retried upload with backoff and succeeded. Verified object integrity via checksum. Storage metrics normalized.
column12: Contact Storage Engineering if failure rate exceeds 5%. Review network path between client and storage region. Update client SDK retry logic recommendations.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge unauthorized access alert from secrets manager; Step 2 - Investigate: Review access logs to identify source IP and identity; Step 3 - Block: Revoke compromised credentials and block source IP; Step 4 - Rotate: Force rotation of all secrets accessed by compromised identity; Step 5 - Report: File security incident report with SOC team.
column5: Revoked compromised IAM credentials and blocked source IP. Rotated all accessed secrets immediately. Notified SOC for further investigation. Security incident report filed.
column12: Immediate escalation to CISO and Security Lead. Initiate breach response protocol. Audit all secrets accessed in last 7 days for potential exposure.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor Elasticsearch cluster health for mapping errors; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify indices with excessive unique field mappings; Phase 3 - Mitigation: Increase mapping limit or consolidate fields; Phase 4 - Recovery: Reindex affected data into new index with correct mapping; Phase 5 - Prevention: Implement field naming conventions to prevent recurrence.
column5: Increased mapping limit temporarily and reindexing data. New index created with consolidated field mappings. Cluster health returned to green. Field naming convention documented.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team if mapping errors persist. Review logging standards to reduce field cardinality. Update index template defaults.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge connection timeout alert from pipeline orchestrator; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check source database health and network connectivity; Step 3 - Remediate: Increase connection timeout settings and retry logic; Step 4 - Verify: Re-run pipeline job and confirm successful extraction; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for recurring timeout issues during peak load.
column5: Increased connection timeout to 60 seconds and added retry logic. Pipeline job completed successfully. Verified data load in target system. Monitoring confirmed stability during peak.
column12: Contact Database Team if timeouts persist during peak. Review source database query performance. Update pipeline error handling documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify primary config server failure via health checks; Phase 2 - Failover: Verify automatic failover to secondary server occurred; Step 3 - Validation: Confirm clients are retrieving config from secondary; Step 4 - Repair: Investigate primary server failure and restore service; Step 5 - Test: Perform failback to primary once repaired.
column5: Automatic failover to secondary succeeded. Clients retrieving config without interruption. Primary server restored and patched. Failback tested successfully. Incident closed.
column12: Escalate to Platform Engineering if failover fails. Review heartbeat configuration between primary and secondary. Update disaster recovery runbook.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge circuit breaker open alert from service mesh; Step 2 - Investigate: Identify downstream service causing failures; Step 3 - Mitigate: Scale downstream service or fix root cause; Step 4 - Reset: Manually reset circuit breaker if auto-reset fails; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch failure rates to ensure circuit stays closed.
column5: Identified downstream database latency issue. Scaled database read replicas. Manually reset circuit breaker. Failure rates dropped below threshold. Circuit remained closed.
column12: Engage Application Team if circuit breaker trips frequently. Review timeout and retry settings in mesh configuration. Update resilience testing scenarios.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect high load on origin shield via metrics; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check cache hit ratio and request patterns; Phase 3 - Scaling: Increase origin shield capacity or add nodes; Phase 4 - Optimization: Tune caching rules to reduce origin requests; Phase 5 - Verification: Confirm origin load returns to normal levels.
column5: Increased origin shield capacity by 50%. Tuned caching rules for dynamic content. Origin load reduced to baseline levels. Verified via monitoring dashboard. Incident resolved.
column12: Contact CDN Provider if scaling limits reached. Review content delivery architecture for optimization. Update capacity planning forecasts.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge PVC pending alert from cluster monitoring; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check storage class provisioner logs for errors; Step 3 - Remediate: Fix storage provisioner configuration or quota issues; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm PVC binds to persistent volume successfully; Step 5 - Monitor: Ensure pod starts and mounts volume correctly.
column5: Fixed storage provisioner configuration quota limit. PVC bound to volume successfully. Pod started and mounted volume. Application data accessible. Incident closed.
column12: Escalate to Storage Team if provisioning fails repeatedly. Review storage quota policies per namespace. Update storage provisioning runbook.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor DLQ size and growth rate via dashboard; Phase 2 - Analysis: Sample messages to identify common failure patterns; Phase 3 - Fix: Update consumer logic to handle malformed messages; Step 4 - Reprocess: Move fixable messages back to main queue for processing; Step 5 - Cleanup: Archive unfixable messages for manual review.
column5: Identified schema mismatch in messages. Updated consumer logic to handle new schema. Reprocessed 5000 messages successfully. Archived 50 unfixable messages. DLQ size normalized.
column12: Engage Data Engineering if DLQ grows rapidly. Implement schema validation at producer level. Update error handling documentation for consumers.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge 503 error rate spike from API Gateway; Step 2 - Triage: Check backend service health endpoints and logs; Step 3 - Mitigate: Enable fallback response or static error page; Step 4 - Recover: Restart backend service or roll back recent deployment; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm API Gateway returns 200 OK responses.
column5: Backend service crashed due to memory leak. Restarted service and rolled back deployment. API Gateway returning 200 OK. Fallback disabled. Stability confirmed.
column12: Immediate escalation to Application Team. Review deployment pipeline for memory testing. Update incident response plan for backend failures.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect replication lag exceeding threshold via metrics; Phase 2 - Investigation: Check network bandwidth and source bucket activity; Phase 3 - Optimization: Increase replication throughput limits; Phase 4 - Verification: Monitor lag reduction until within SLA; Phase 5 - Documentation: Record replication performance baseline.
column5: Increased replication throughput limits. Network bandwidth optimized. Replication lag reduced to under 5 minutes. SLA met. Performance baseline documented.
column12: Contact Storage Provider if lag persists. Review replication rules for unnecessary filters. Update disaster recovery RPO expectations.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge rate limit exceeded alert from secrets manager; Step 2 - Diagnose: Identify application causing excessive secret requests; Step 3 - Mitigate: Implement client-side caching for secrets; Step 4 - Request: Apply for rate limit increase if legitimate need; Step 5 - Monitor: Verify API call rate returns to normal.
column5: Identified misconfigured application requesting secrets every second. Implemented client-side caching. API call rate reduced by 90%. Rate limit increase not needed. Monitoring confirmed.
column12: Engage Application Team to fix secret retrieval logic. Review secret TTL settings to encourage caching. Update development guidelines for secret usage.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify offline agents via monitoring dashboard; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check agent service status and network connectivity; Step 3 - Remediate: Restart agent service and verify configuration; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm logs resumes flowing to aggregator; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch agent stability over next 24 hours.
column5: Agent service stopped due to memory leak. Restarted service and increased memory limit. Logs resumed flowing successfully. Agent stable over 24 hours. Incident closed.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team if agent crashes repeatedly. Review agent version for known bugs. Update agent deployment automation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge data quality alert from pipeline framework; Step 2 - Analyze: Review failed quality rules and sample data; Step 3 - Fix: Correct source data or update quality rules; Step 4 - Replay: Re-run pipeline stage with corrected data; Step 5 - Validate: Confirm downstream reports reflect correct data.
column5: Identified null values in required field. Fixed source data generation logic. Re-ran pipeline stage successfully. Downstream reports updated. Data quality check passed.
column12: Notify Data Owners of quality issue. Implement stricter validation at ingestion layer. Update data quality rule documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Planning: Schedule encryption key rotation during maintenance window; Step 2 - Execution: Generate new encryption keys and update config server; Step 3 - Re-encrypt: Re-encrypt all stored configurations with new key; Step 4 - Verification: Confirm clients can decrypt configurations successfully; Step 5 - Cleanup: Securely delete old encryption keys.
column5: Generated new encryption keys and updated config server. All configurations re-encrypted successfully. Clients decrypted configs without error. Old keys destroyed securely. Audit log updated.
column12: Escalate to Security Team if decryption fails. Ensure backup of keys before rotation. Update security compliance documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge MTLS handshake failure alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check certificate validity and trust chain on both sides; Step 3 - Remediate: Renew or replace mismatched certificates; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm service-to-service communication restored; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for further MTLS errors in mesh telemetry.
column5: Identified expired certificate on service A. Renewed certificate and updated trust store. MTLS handshakes successful. Service communication restored. No further errors observed.
column12: Escalate to Security Team if certificate issues persist. Implement automated certificate management for mesh. Update MTLS configuration runbook.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify blocked legitimate traffic via WAF logs; Step 2 - Analysis: Review WAF rule triggering the block; Step 3 - Mitigation: Add exception rule for legitimate traffic pattern; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm legitimate traffic passes through WAF; Step 5 - Tune: Adjust WAF rule sensitivity to prevent future false positives.
column5: Identified WAF rule blocking valid API requests. Added exception rule for specific user agent. Legitimate traffic passing through. WAF rule sensitivity adjusted. False positives reduced.
column12: Contact Security Team before disabling WAF rules. Review WAF rule updates before deployment. Update WAF tuning documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge HPA not scaling alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check metrics server availability and HPA configuration; Step 3 - Remediate: Fix metrics server or adjust HPA thresholds; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm HPA scales pods based on load; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch scaling events for correctness.
column5: Metrics server was down causing HPA failure. Restarted metrics server. HPA scaled pods correctly based on CPU usage. Scaling events logged correctly. Incident resolved.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if metrics server fails repeatedly. Implement redundant metrics servers. Update autoscaling runbook.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect low disk space alert on message broker; Step 2 - Investigation: Check log retention policies and queue depths; Step 3 - Cleanup: Delete old logs or increase disk volume; Step 4 - Verification: Confirm disk usage below threshold; Step 5 - Prevention: Adjust retention policies to prevent recurrence.
column5: Deleted old broker logs to free space. Disk usage reduced to 60%. Retention policy adjusted to 7 days. Monitoring confirmed stable disk usage. Incident closed.
column12: Escalate to Infrastructure Team if disk fills rapidly. Implement automated disk cleanup scripts. Update capacity planning for broker storage.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge increased latency alert from API Gateway; Step 2 - Triage: Identify specific endpoints causing timeouts; Step 3 - Investigate: Check backend performance and network latency; Step 4 - Adjust: Increase timeout settings temporarily; Step 5 - Fix: Optimize backend code or database queries.
column5: Identified slow database query causing timeouts. Optimized query with index. Timeout settings reverted to normal. Latency returned to baseline. Backend performance improved.
column12: Engage Application Team for code optimization. Review database indexing strategy. Update API SLA documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor access denied error rates via storage metrics; Step 2 - Analysis: Check IAM policies and bucket permissions; Step 3 - Remediate: Correct IAM policy or bucket ACL; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm applications can access storage successfully; Step 5 - Audit: Review permission changes to prevent recurrence.
column5: Identified incorrect IAM policy deployed recently. Corrected policy to allow access. Applications accessing storage successfully. Permission change audited. Incident resolved.
column12: Escalate to Security Team if access denied persists. Implement IAM policy validation in CI/CD. Update access control documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge secret version limit warning; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check secret version history and retention settings; Step 3 - Cleanup: Delete old secret versions to free slots; Step 4 - Configure: Adjust version retention policy; Step 5 - Monitor: Verify new rotations succeed without errors.
column5: Deleted old secret versions older than 90 days. Adjusted retention policy to keep 10 versions. New rotations succeeding. Monitoring confirmed version count stable. Incident closed.
column12: Contact Security Team before deleting versions. Automate version cleanup via lambda function. Update secret management guidelines.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect slow query alerts from log analytics; Step 2 - Analysis: Identify expensive queries and scan patterns; Step 3 - Optimization: Add indexes or optimize query syntax; Step 4 - Verification: Confirm query latency returns to normal; Step 5 - Documentation: Update query best practices guide.
column5: Identified full table scan causing slowness. Added index on timestamp field. Query latency reduced from 10s to 200ms. Best practices guide updated. Incident resolved.
column12: Engage Observability Team for schema optimization. Implement query linting in development. Update performance tuning documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge schema registry connectivity alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check schema registry service health and network; Step 3 - Remediate: Restart schema registry or failover to backup; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm producers can register schemas successfully; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for connectivity issues over 24 hours.
column5: Schema registry service crashed. Restarted service successfully. Producers registering schemas without error. Backup failover tested. Stability confirmed over 24 hours.
column12: Escalate to Data Platform Team if registry fails repeatedly. Implement high availability for registry. Update schema management runbook.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify config sync failure via dashboard; Step 2 - Investigation: Check git credentials and repository access; Step 3 - Fix: Update credentials or fix repository permissions; Step 4 - Retry: Trigger manual sync and verify success; Step 5 - Validate: Confirm clients receive latest configuration.
column5: Git credentials expired causing sync failure. Updated credentials in config server. Manual sync succeeded. Clients received latest config. Incident closed.
column12: Escalate to DevOps Team if sync fails again. Implement automated credential rotation. Update git integration documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge traffic shifting failure alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check virtual service configuration and weights; Step 3 - Remediate: Correct traffic weights and apply configuration; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm traffic splits according to plan; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for errors in shifted traffic.
column5: Traffic weights misconfigured in virtual service. Corrected weights to 90/10 split. Traffic shifting as expected. No errors in shifted traffic. Incident resolved.
column12: Engage Platform Team before changing traffic weights. Implement canary deployment validation. Update traffic management runbook.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect edge node offline via CDN health checks; Step 2 - Investigation: Check node hardware and network connectivity; Step 3 - Mitigation: Route traffic to healthy edge nodes; Step 4 - Repair: Replace or repair failed edge node; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm node returns to service pool.
column5: Edge node hardware failure detected. Traffic routed to healthy nodes automatically. Node replaced by provider. Node returned to service pool. Performance normalized.
column12: Contact CDN Provider for hardware replacement. Review edge node redundancy settings. Update CDN incident response plan.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge high CPU usage on ingress controller; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check traffic volume and SSL termination load; Step 3 - Scale: Add additional ingress controller replicas; Step 4 - Optimize: Tune SSL cipher suites for performance; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm CPU usage returns to normal.
column5: High traffic causing CPU saturation. Scaled ingress controller to 3 replicas. Tuned SSL cipher suites. CPU usage dropped to 40%. Performance stable.
column12: Escalate to Network Team if CPU remains high. Consider dedicated load balancer. Update ingress capacity planning.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify producer connection errors via logs; Step 2 - Investigation: Check broker listener status and firewall rules; Step 3 - Remediate: Open firewall ports or restart listener; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm producers can connect successfully; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for connection stability.
column5: Firewall rule blocked producer IP range. Updated firewall rules to allow traffic. Producers connected successfully. Connection stability confirmed. Incident closed.
column12: Escalate to Network Security Team immediately. Review firewall change management process. Update network access documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge auth timeout alert from API Gateway; Step 2 - Triage: Check authentication service health and latency; Step 3 - Mitigate: Enable caching for tokens; Step 4 - Recover: Restart auth service or scale resources; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm authentication success rate returns to normal.
column5: Auth service overloaded causing timeouts. Scaled auth service horizontally. Enabled token caching. Authentication success rate 99.9%. Incident resolved.
column12: Engage Security Team for auth service scaling. Implement circuit breaker for auth calls. Update authentication architecture diagram.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect lifecycle policy execution failure; Step 2 - Analysis: Check policy syntax and bucket permissions; Step 3 - Fix: Correct policy syntax and reapply; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm objects transitioned or expired as expected; Step 5 - Audit: Review lifecycle policies for compliance.
column5: Lifecycle policy syntax error prevented execution. Corrected syntax and reapplied policy. Objects transitioned to cold storage. Compliance audit passed. Incident closed.
column12: Contact Storage Team if policy fails repeatedly. Implement policy validation in CI/CD. Update lifecycle management documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge plugin failure alert from secrets manager; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check plugin logs and compatibility version; Step 3 - Remediate: Update plugin to compatible version; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm integration functions correctly; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for plugin stability over time.
column5: Plugin version incompatible with secrets manager. Updated plugin to latest version. Integration functioning correctly. Stability confirmed over 48 hours. Incident resolved.
column12: Escalate to Vendor if plugin issues persist. Test plugins in staging before production. Update integration compatibility matrix.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify storage capacity warning via monitoring; Step 2 - Investigation: Check log retention and index sizes; Step 3 - Cleanup: Delete old indices or expand storage; Step 4 - Verification: Confirm storage usage below threshold; Step 5 - Planning: Update capacity forecast based on growth.
column5: Storage 85% full due to log growth. Deleted indices older than 30 days. Storage usage reduced to 60%. Capacity forecast updated. Incident closed.
column12: Engage Observability Team for retention policy review. Implement automated index lifecycle management. Update storage procurement plan.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge checkpoint failure alert from pipeline; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check state backend connectivity and permissions; Step 3 - Remediate: Fix state backend configuration; Step 4 - Recover: Restore from last successful checkpoint; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm pipeline resumes processing correctly.
column5: State backend connectivity lost due to network issue. Fixed network configuration. Restored from checkpoint successfully. Pipeline processing resumed. Incident resolved.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if checkpointing fails often. Implement redundant state backend. Update pipeline reliability documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect slow config retrieval times; Step 2 - Analysis: Check server load and database performance; Step 3 - Optimization: Enable caching and tune database queries; Step 4 - Verification: Confirm retrieval times within SLA; Step 5 - Documentation: Record performance tuning changes.
column5: Database queries causing slow config retrieval. Enabled Redis caching for configs. Retrieval times reduced to <50ms. SLA met. Tuning changes documented.
column12: Engage Platform Team for database optimization. Implement config client-side caching. Update performance baseline documentation.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge missing trace data alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check tracing agent and collector connectivity; Step 3 - Remediate: Restart tracing components and fix config; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm traces appearing in dashboard; Step 5 - Monitor: Watch for data completeness over time.
column5: Tracing collector crashed due to memory issue. Restarted collector and increased memory. Traces appearing in dashboard. Data completeness confirmed. Incident resolved.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team if data loss recurs. Implement high availability for collectors. Update tracing architecture documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify high SSL failure rate via CDN metrics; Step 2 - Analysis: Check SSL protocol versions and cipher compatibility; Step 3 - Mitigation: Enable broader cipher support temporarily; Step 4 - Fix: Update client configurations or CDN SSL policy; Step 5 - Verify: Confirm handshake success rate returns to normal.
column5: Old clients incompatible with TLS 1.3 only policy. Enabled TLS 1.2 support temporarily. Handshake success rate 99.9%. Client update campaign initiated. Incident resolved.
column12: Contact Security Team before lowering TLS versions. Plan client deprecation strategy. Update SSL configuration standards.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor namespace resource quota alerts via Prometheus; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify which resources (CPU/memory/pods) are exhausted; Phase 3 - Investigation: List all pods in namespace and identify resource hogs; Phase 4 - Remediation: Request quota increase or terminate non-essential pods; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm new pods can be scheduled successfully; Phase 6 - Documentation: Update capacity planning documents.
column5: Requested quota increase from 10 to 20 CPU cores. Terminated 3 non-essential development pods. New production pods scheduled successfully. Capacity planning updated with new baseline. Incident closed after stakeholder notification.
column12: Escalate to Platform Engineering if quota increase denied. Review namespace isolation policies. Mandatory capacity review within 48 hours. Update quota management runbook K8S-QUOTA-001.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge schema validation failure alert from message broker; Step 2 - Triage: Identify affected topics and message types; Step 3 - Diagnosis: Compare message schema against registered schema in schema registry; Step 4 - Fix: Update producer to use correct schema version; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm messages are being validated successfully; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch for schema drift over next 24 hours.
column5: Updated producer application to use schema version 2.1. Schema validation passing for all new messages. Reprocessed 200 failed messages from dead letter queue. Schema registry updated with new version. Monitoring confirmed no further validation failures.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if schema failures persist. Implement schema compatibility checks in CI/CD pipeline. Update schema evolution documentation. Notify all downstream consumers of schema change.

column4: Procedure 1 - Identify: Check API Gateway logs for CORS preflight failures; Procedure 2 - Analysis: Review current CORS policy against application requirements; Procedure 3 - Correction: Update CORS allowed origins and methods; Procedure 4 - Testing: Verify cross-origin requests succeed from all client domains; Procedure 5 - Validation: Run automated CORS test suite; Procedure 6 - Closure: Document policy changes in API governance portal.
column5: Updated CORS policy to include new frontend domain. Added PUT and DELETE methods to allowed list. All cross-origin requests now succeeding. Automated test suite passed. Changes documented in API governance portal with approval from security team.
column12: Security team must approve all CORS policy changes. Ensure no wildcard origins in production. Update API security guidelines. Review CORS policy quarterly for compliance.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Review storage configuration change alerts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Compare current bucket configuration against baseline; Phase 3 - Investigation: Check CloudTrail for who enabled versioning; Phase 4 - Correction: Disable versioning if not required or configure lifecycle rules; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm storage costs stabilize; Phase 6 - Documentation: Update bucket configuration standards.
column5: Versioning was enabled by mistake during migration. Disabled versioning and configured lifecycle rule to delete old versions after 30 days. Storage cost projection stabilized. Change traced to deployment script bug. Script fixed and bucket standards updated.
column12: Contact Storage Team before changing versioning settings. Implement configuration drift detection. Update bucket provisioning templates. Review storage cost impact weekly.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge audit logging failure alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check CloudTrail configuration and S3 bucket permissions; Step 3 - Fix: Restore audit log bucket policy and IAM permissions; Step 4 - Verify: Confirm audit events are being recorded; Step 5 - Review: Analyze missed audit events for security implications; Step 6 - Report: File compliance report for audit gap period.
column5: Audit log bucket policy was modified incorrectly. Restored proper bucket policy and IAM role permissions. Audit events now recording successfully. Reviewed 4-hour gap with no suspicious activity. Compliance report filed with security team.
column12: Immediate escalation to Security Team required. Audit gaps must be reported within 24 hours. Implement audit log integrity monitoring. Update compliance documentation.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect cluster health degradation via Kibana dashboard; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify unassigned shards and root cause; Phase 3 - Investigation: Check node disk space and memory pressure; Phase 4 - Remediation: Add data nodes or adjust replica count; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm cluster returns to green status; Phase 6 - Optimization: Tune shard allocation settings for stability.
column5: Two replica shards unassigned due to insufficient nodes. Added one data node to cluster. Cluster returned to green status within 30 minutes. Adjusted shard allocation awareness settings. Monitoring confirmed stable cluster health.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team if cluster stays yellow >1 hour. Review shard sizing strategy. Update capacity planning for log volume growth. Implement automated node scaling.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge checkpoint failure alert from stream processor; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check state backend connectivity and permissions; Step 3 - Investigation: Review checkpoint storage for corruption; Step 4 - Recovery: Restore from last successful checkpoint; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm stream processing resumes correctly; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch checkpoint success rate over 24 hours.
column5: Checkpoint storage bucket had permission change. Restored proper IAM permissions. Job restored from checkpoint 15 minutes ago. Processing resumed with no data loss. Checkpoint success rate 100% over next 24 hours.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if checkpoint failures recur. Implement redundant checkpoint storage. Update streaming job reliability documentation. Review state backend architecture.

column4: Phase 1 - Planning: Schedule key rotation during low-traffic maintenance window; Phase 2 - Backup: Export all encrypted configurations with current key; Phase 3 - Rotation: Generate new encryption key and update config server; Phase 4 - Re-encryption: Re-encrypt all configurations with new key; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm all clients can decrypt configurations; Phase 6 - Cleanup: Securely destroy old encryption key and update key management system.
column5: Key rotation completed during 2 AM maintenance window. All 500+ configurations re-encrypted successfully. All clients decrypted configs without errors. Old key destroyed via HSM. Key rotation documented in security audit log.
column12: Security Team must approve and witness key rotation. Maintain key backup in secure offline storage. Update encryption key rotation schedule to quarterly. Document in compliance evidence repository.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge service registration failure alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check service registry health and network connectivity; Step 3 - Investigation: Review service agent logs for registration errors; Step 4 - Remediation: Restart service agent and verify DNS resolution; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm service appears in service mesh dashboard; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch registration success rate for all services.
column5: Service registry was overloaded causing registration timeouts. Scaled registry to 3 nodes. Restarted affected service agents. All services now registered successfully. Registration success rate 99.9% over monitoring period.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if registry overload persists. Implement service registration health checks. Update service mesh capacity planning. Review service discovery architecture.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor CDN origin timeout metrics via analytics dashboard; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify which edge locations experiencing timeouts; Phase 3 - Investigation: Check origin server health and network path; Phase 4 - Optimization: Increase origin timeout settings and enable connection pooling; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm timeout rates return to baseline; Phase 6 - Documentation: Update CDN configuration standards.
column5: Origin timeouts increased from 0.1% to 2% in APAC region. Origin server was under-provisioned. Scaled origin servers and increased CDN timeout to 30 seconds. Timeout rates returned to 0.1%. Configuration updated in CDN management portal.
column12: Contact CDN Provider if timeouts persist after origin scaling. Review origin capacity for geographic distribution. Update CDN SLA monitoring thresholds. Document timeout tuning procedures.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge certificate renewal failure from cert-manager; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check cert-manager logs and ACME challenge status; Step 3 - Investigation: Verify DNS records for HTTP-01 or DNS-01 challenge; Step 4 - Fix: Correct DNS configuration and retry certificate issuance; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm new certificate is installed on ingress; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch certificate expiry alerts for all domains.
column5: DNS-01 challenge failing due to TXT record propagation delay. Increased TTL and waited for propagation. Certificate issued successfully and installed on ingress. All domains now serving valid certificates. Certificate expiry monitoring confirmed.
column12: Escalate to Security Team if certificate expires. Implement certificate expiry alerts at 30/14/7 days. Update cert-manager configuration for retry logic. Document DNS provider integration requirements.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify frequent rebalancing events via broker metrics; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check consumer heartbeat intervals and session timeouts; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review consumer application logs for processing delays; Phase 4 - Tuning: Adjust heartbeat interval and max poll interval; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm rebalancing frequency decreases; Phase 6 - Optimization: Implement graceful shutdown handling in consumers.
column5: Consumers were timing out during long processing causing rebalancing. Increased session timeout from 10s to 30s. Implemented graceful shutdown with partition revocation listener. Rebalancing events reduced from hourly to daily. Consumer stability confirmed.
column12: Escalate to Messaging Team if rebalancing impacts throughput. Review consumer processing time SLAs. Update consumer development guidelines. Implement rebalancing alerting thresholds.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge 413 error rate spike from API Gateway; Step 2 - Triage: Identify which endpoints receiving large payloads; Step 3 - Analysis: Compare payload sizes against configured limits; Step 4 - Adjustment: Increase body size limit for specific endpoints if legitimate; Step 5 - Validation: Test large payload requests succeed; Step 6 - Documentation: Update API documentation with new limits.
column5: File upload endpoint receiving payloads larger than 10MB limit. Increased limit to 50MB for upload endpoint only. Other endpoints remain at 10MB. Load testing confirmed 50MB uploads succeed. API documentation updated with endpoint-specific limits.
column12: Security team must approve body size limit increases. Implement per-endpoint rate limiting. Update API gateway WAF rules. Document payload size standards in API guidelines.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor access denied errors from cross-account roles; Phase 2 - Analysis: Review IAM trust policies and bucket policies; Phase 3 - Investigation: Check role ARN and external ID configuration; Phase 4 - Correction: Update trust policy to include correct account ID; Phase 5 - Verification: Confirm cross-account access works; Phase 6 - Audit: Review all cross-account access configurations.
column5: Cross-account role trust policy had incorrect account ID. Updated trust policy with correct 12-digit account ID. Cross-account access now working. Audited 15 other cross-account roles for similar issues. All corrected and documented.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for cross-account access issues. Implement IAM access analyzer. Update cross-account access templates. Quarterly review of all cross-account permissions required.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge Lambda timeout during secret rotation; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check Lambda execution logs and duration; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify which step in rotation is slow; Step 4 - Optimization: Increase Lambda timeout and memory; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm rotation completes successfully; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch rotation duration for future runs.
column5: Lambda was timing out at 60 seconds during database password update. Increased timeout to 300 seconds and memory to 512MB. Rotation completed in 45 seconds. All dependent applications updated credentials successfully. Rotation schedule confirmed.
column12: Escalate to Security Engineering if rotation fails twice. Review Lambda cold start impact. Update rotation function with better error handling. Implement rotation success alerting.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect parsing failures via log quality metrics; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify which log format causing parse errors; Phase 3 - Investigation: Test grok patterns against sample logs; Phase 4 - Fix: Update grok pattern to handle new log format; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm parsing success rate improves; Phase 6 - Documentation: Update log format specification.
column5: Application deployed new log format breaking existing grok pattern. Updated grok pattern to match new timestamp and level format. Parsing success rate improved from 60% to 99%. Log format change communicated to all teams. Pattern added to shared grok library.
column12: Contact application team before log format changes. Implement log format validation in CI/CD. Update log standardization guidelines. Maintain grok pattern version control.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge schema compatibility failure alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check schema registry for backward compatibility errors; Step 3 - Investigation: Compare new schema against previous versions; Step 4 - Remediation: Fix schema to maintain backward compatibility; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm schema registered successfully; Step 6 - Communication: Notify all consumers of schema update.
column5: New schema removed required field breaking backward compatibility. Added field as optional with default value. Schema registered with BACKWARD compatibility. All existing consumers continue working. Schema change documented in data catalog.
column12: Escalate to Data Governance if compatibility cannot be maintained. Implement schema compatibility gates in CI/CD. Update schema evolution policy. Require consumer notification for breaking changes.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Identify both config servers claiming primary role; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check consensus algorithm and network partition; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review split-brain detection logs; Phase 4 - Resolution: Force one node to secondary and restore consensus; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm single primary elected; Phase 6 - Prevention: Tune split-brain detection thresholds.
column5: Network partition caused both nodes to claim primary. Restored network connectivity and forced node 2 to secondary. Consensus restored with node 1 as primary. Tuned heartbeat timeout from 5s to 3s. Split-brain detection improved.
column12: Immediate escalation to Platform Engineering. Split-brain can cause configuration inconsistency. Review network redundancy. Implement automated split-brain resolution. Post-incident review mandatory.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge rate limit bypass detection; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check rate limiting configuration and policy attachment; Step 3 - Investigation: Verify policy is applied to correct services; Step 4 - Fix: Re-apply rate limiting policy with correct selectors; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm rate limiting is enforced; Step 6 - Testing: Run load test to validate rate limits.
column5: Rate limiting policy had incorrect service selector. Updated selector to match service labels. Rate limiting now enforced at 1000 requests per second. Load test confirmed requests above limit are rejected. Policy attachment verified in mesh dashboard.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for rate limit bypass. Review all rate limiting policies for correctness. Implement policy validation in deployment pipeline. Update rate limiting standards.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor cache purge completion status; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check purge API response and job status; Phase 3 - Investigation: Verify purge request reached all edge locations; Phase 4 - Remediation: Retry purge with invalidation pattern; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm cached content updated at edge; Phase 6 - Documentation: Record purge latency metrics.
column5: Cache purge stuck at 80% completion for 2 hours. Retried purge with specific path pattern instead of wildcard. Purge completed in 15 minutes. Verified content updated via edge location testing. Purge latency documented for future SLA.
column12: Contact CDN Provider if purge exceeds 30 minutes. Implement purge status monitoring. Update content deployment procedures. Consider versioned URLs instead of purges.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge pod stuck in Terminating state; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check pod finalizers and volume attachments; Step 3 - Investigation: Review kubelet logs on node hosting pod; Step 4 - Resolution: Remove finalizer manually or force delete pod; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm StatefulSet creates replacement pod; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch for recurring termination issues.
column5: Pod stuck due to volume attachment not releasing. Removed finalizer manually using kubectl patch. StatefulSet created replacement pod successfully. Volume reattached to new pod. No recurring issues over 24 hours.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if volume attachment issues persist. Review storage class configuration. Update StatefulSet troubleshooting runbook. Implement pod termination timeout alerting.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect duplicate message processing via application logs; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check deduplication key generation logic; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review message ID format and uniqueness; Step 4 - Fix: Implement proper deduplication using message ID; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm no duplicate processing; Step 6 - Documentation: Update message handling guidelines.
column5: Application was not using message ID for deduplication. Implemented idempotency key using message ID and timestamp. Duplicate processing eliminated. Added deduplication test to integration test suite. Guidelines updated for all consumers.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if duplicates cause data issues. Implement exactly-once processing where possible. Update message consumer templates. Review deduplication strategy per use case.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge 401 errors from JWT validation; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check JWT issuer configuration in API Gateway; Step 3 - Investigation: Verify identity provider issuer URL; Step 4 - Fix: Update issuer URL to match identity provider; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm JWT validation succeeds; Step 6 - Testing: Test authentication flow end-to-end.
column5: Identity provider changed issuer URL without notification. Updated API Gateway JWT authorizer with new issuer URL. JWT validation passing for all requests. End-to-end authentication tested successfully. Added issuer change monitoring.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for identity provider changes. Implement issuer URL monitoring. Update identity provider change management process. Document JWT configuration requirements.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor lifecycle policy execution status; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check which objects failed to transition; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review storage class compatibility and object lock status; Phase 4 - Remediation: Fix lifecycle policy and retry transition; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm objects moved to correct storage class; Phase 6 - Optimization: Update lifecycle policy for edge cases.
column5: Objects with retention lock could not transition to Glacier. Updated lifecycle policy to exclude locked objects. 10000 objects transitioned successfully. Policy updated to handle retention scenarios. Cost savings realized as expected.
column12: Contact Storage Team if transition failure rate >5%. Review object retention policies. Update lifecycle policy templates. Monitor storage class distribution monthly.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge access denied due to resource policy; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check secret resource policy and IAM policy; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify conflicting deny statements; Step 4 - Resolution: Remove or modify conflicting policy statement; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm authorized access restored; Step 6 - Audit: Review all secret policies for conflicts.
column5: Resource policy had deny statement blocking legitimate role. Removed overly broad deny statement. Authorized access restored for application roles. Audited 50 secrets for similar policy issues. 3 secrets corrected. Policy review process updated.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for policy conflicts. Implement policy validation before deployment. Update secret policy templates. Quarterly policy audit required.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect ILM policy execution failures; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check which indices failed to rollover; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review ILM policy and index template; Step 4 - Fix: Correct ILM policy configuration and retry; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm indices rolling over correctly; Step 6 - Documentation: Update ILM policy documentation.
column5: ILM policy had incorrect rollover size threshold. Updated from 50GB to 30GB based on shard sizing. Indices now rolling over correctly. Shard count optimized. ILM policy documented with sizing rationale.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team if ILM failures persist. Review index sizing quarterly. Update ILM policy with capacity planning. Implement ILM success alerting.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge schema drift detection from connector; Step 2 - Diagnose: Compare source schema with registered schema; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify which columns changed; Step 4 - Remediation: Update connector configuration for new schema; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm data flowing correctly; Step 6 - Communication: Notify downstream consumers of schema change.
column5: Source database added new column without notification. Updated connector to handle schema evolution. Data flowing correctly with new column. Downstream consumers notified. Schema change process reinforced with source team.
column12: Escalate to Data Governance for unannounced schema changes. Implement schema change detection alerting. Update source system change management. Document schema evolution procedures.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor configuration deployment failures; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check validation errors from config server; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review configuration syntax and schema; Step 4 - Fix: Correct configuration errors and redeploy; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm configuration applied successfully; Step 6 - Prevention: Add configuration validation to CI/CD.
column5: Configuration had invalid JSON syntax causing deployment failure. Fixed syntax error and added JSON schema validation. Configuration deployed successfully. Added pre-deployment validation to CI/CD pipeline. No further validation failures.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if validation bypassed. Implement configuration linting. Update configuration standards. Require peer review for config changes.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge circuit breaker opening unexpectedly; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check error rate threshold and window size; Step 3 - Investigation: Review actual error rates vs threshold; Step 4 - Tuning: Adjust circuit breaker sensitivity; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm circuit stays closed during normal operation; Step 6 - Documentation: Update circuit breaker configuration guidelines.
column5: Circuit breaker threshold too sensitive at 5% error rate. Increased to 10% with 1-minute window. Circuit no longer opening falsely. Tested with simulated failures. Guidelines updated with tuning recommendations.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if circuit breaker impacts availability. Review error rate baselines. Update resilience testing scenarios. Document circuit breaker tuning procedures.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor blocked request patterns for bypass attempts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify bypass method (proxy/VPN/DNS); Phase 3 - Investigation: Check geo-restriction configuration; Step 4 - Mitigation: Strengthen geo-restriction rules; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm bypass attempts blocked; Step 6 - Reporting: Report bypass attempts to security team.
column5: Users bypassing geo-restriction via VPN endpoints. Updated geo-restriction to block known VPN IP ranges. Bypass attempts reduced by 90%. Security team notified of bypass patterns. Geo-restriction effectiveness monitored.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for bypass attempts. Review geo-restriction compliance requirements. Update CDN security configuration. Document bypass detection procedures.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge pending pods due to insufficient capacity; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check autoscaler logs for scaling decisions; Step 3 - Investigation: Verify node group configuration and limits; Step 4 - Fix: Increase node group max size or fix autoscaler config; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm new nodes provisioned; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch autoscaler metrics for correctness.
column5: Autoscaler hit node group max size of 10 nodes. Increased max to 20 nodes. 5 new nodes provisioned within 10 minutes. Pending pods scheduled successfully. Autoscaler metrics confirmed healthy.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if autoscaler fails repeatedly. Review node group sizing strategy. Update autoscaler configuration documentation. Implement capacity forecasting.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor broker upgrade progress and errors; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check producer/consumer compatibility with new version; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review upgrade release notes for breaking changes; Phase 4 - Remediation: Update clients or rollback broker version; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm message flow restored; Step 6 - Documentation: Update upgrade compatibility matrix.
column5: Broker upgrade to 3.0 broke 2.x client compatibility. Rolled back to 2.8 and updated clients to 3.x. Message flow restored. Compatibility matrix updated. Upgrade process revised to include client testing.
column12: Immediate escalation to Messaging Platform Team. Test all clients before broker upgrades. Maintain version compatibility documentation. Implement canary upgrade strategy.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge connection timeout errors from API Gateway; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check connection pool metrics and utilization; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify slow backend causing pool hold; Step 4 - Remediation: Increase pool size and optimize backend; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm connection errors resolved; Step 6 - Optimization: Implement connection pool monitoring.
column5: Backend latency causing connections to hold too long. Increased pool size from 50 to 200 connections. Optimized backend query reducing response time. Connection errors eliminated. Pool monitoring dashboard created.
column12: Escalate to Application Team for backend optimization. Review connection pool sizing per backend. Update API Gateway capacity planning. Implement pool exhaustion alerting.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor incomplete multipart upload alerts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Identify which upload parts are missing; Phase 3 - Investigation: Check network stability during upload; Step 4 - Recovery: Abort incomplete upload and retry; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm upload completes successfully; Step 6 - Prevention: Implement upload retry logic.
column5: Network interruption caused part 5 of 10 to fail. Aborted incomplete upload and retried with exponential backoff. Upload completed successfully. Client SDK updated with retry logic. Upload success rate improved to 99.9%.
column12: Contact Storage Team if missing parts exceed 5%. Review network path for stability. Update upload client recommendations. Implement multipart upload monitoring.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge unexpected access denial; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check policy inheritance from parent resource; Step 3 - Investigation: Review IAM policy evaluation logic; Step 4 - Fix: Add explicit allow statement to secret policy; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm access restored for authorized principals; Step 6 - Audit: Review policy inheritance for other secrets.
column5: Parent resource policy was denying access despite secret allow policy. Added explicit allow statement to secret policy. Access restored for application roles. Audited 30 secrets for similar issues. Policy inheritance documented.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for policy inheritance issues. Implement policy simulation before deployment. Update IAM policy guidelines. Quarterly policy review required.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect buffer overflow alerts from log shipper; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check ingestion rate vs shipper capacity; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review shipper memory and disk buffer settings; Step 4 - Remediation: Increase buffer size and add shippers; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm no log loss during peak; Step 6 - Optimization: Implement adaptive buffering.
column5: Log volume spike caused buffer overflow and log loss. Increased buffer from 1GB to 5GB. Added 2 additional shippers. No log loss during subsequent peak. Adaptive buffering implemented based on queue depth.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team if log loss occurs. Review log volume forecasting. Update shipper capacity planning. Implement log loss alerting.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge watermark not advancing alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check event time distribution and late arrivals; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify partition causing watermark stall; Step 4 - Fix: Adjust allowed lateness and watermark strategy; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm watermark advancing correctly; Step 6 - Documentation: Update streaming job configuration.
column5: Single partition with late events blocking watermark. Adjusted allowed lateness from 1 minute to 5 minutes. Watermark advancing correctly. Results now complete with 5-minute delay. Configuration documented.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if watermark stalls >30 minutes. Review event time semantics. Update streaming job patterns. Implement watermark monitoring dashboard.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor configuration change propagation; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check cache invalidation events and timing; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review cache TTL and invalidation triggers; Step 4 - Fix: Force cache refresh and fix invalidation logic; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm clients receive updated configuration; Step 6 - Prevention: Add cache health monitoring.
column5: Cache invalidation event not triggering after config update. Forced cache refresh and fixed webhook trigger. Clients received updated config within 1 minute. Cache health monitoring added. Invalidation tested in staging.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if cache issues persist. Review cache invalidation architecture. Update configuration propagation SLA. Implement cache hit rate monitoring.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge uneven traffic distribution alert; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check load balancing algorithm and weights; Step 3 - Investigation: Analyze traffic distribution across instances; Step 4 - Optimization: Switch to least-connections algorithm; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm traffic balanced correctly; Step 6 - Documentation: Update load balancing standards.
column5: Round-robin causing imbalance with varying request durations. Switched to least-connections algorithm. Traffic distribution improved from 60/40 to 52/48. Response times more consistent. Load balancing standards updated.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if imbalance affects performance. Review algorithm per service pattern. Update service mesh configuration guidelines. Implement traffic distribution monitoring.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor edge function execution timeouts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check function duration and complexity; Phase 3 - Investigation: Identify which operations causing delay; Step 4 - Optimization: Reduce function complexity or increase timeout; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm function completes within limits; Step 6 - Documentation: Update edge function best practices.
column5: Edge function doing synchronous external API call causing timeout. Refactored to async with cached responses. Function now completes in 50ms. Timeout limit increased to 5 seconds. Best practices updated.
column12: Contact CDN Provider if timeout limit insufficient. Review edge function architecture. Update function development guidelines. Implement function performance monitoring.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge connectivity failure between pods; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check network policy rules and selectors; Step 3 - Investigation: Test connectivity with policy disabled; Step 4 - Fix: Update network policy to allow required traffic; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm pods can communicate; Step 6 - Testing: Add network policy tests to CI/CD.
column5: Network policy too restrictive blocking service-to-service traffic. Added egress rule for required port and namespace. Pods communicating successfully. Network policy tests added to CI/CD. Policy review process updated.
column12: Escalate to Security Team for network policy changes. Implement network policy validation. Update network segmentation documentation. Require network policy peer review.

column4: Phase 1 - Alert: Acknowledge consumer processing old messages; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check consumer group offset and lag; Step 3 - Investigation: Determine correct offset position; Step 4 - Action: Reset offset to latest or specific timestamp; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm consumer processing new messages; Step 6 - Documentation: Record offset reset in change log.
column5: Consumer was reprocessing messages from 24 hours ago. Reset offset to latest position. Consumer now processing new messages only. Offset reset documented with justification. Consumer group monitoring enhanced.
column12: Escalate to Messaging Team before offset reset. Document business impact of message loss. Update offset management procedures. Implement offset change alerting.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge transformation failure errors; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check transformation script and input format; Step 3 - Investigation: Test transformation with sample requests; Step 4 - Fix: Correct transformation logic and redeploy; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm requests transformed correctly; Step 6 - Testing: Add transformation test cases.
column5: Transformation script had null pointer on missing header. Added null check and default value. Requests transforming correctly. Test cases added for edge cases. Transformation library updated.
column12: Escalate to API Team for transformation changes. Implement transformation validation. Update request transformation guidelines. Add transformation monitoring.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor replication conflict alerts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check conflicting object versions; Phase 3 - Investigation: Review replication rule priority; Step 4 - Resolution: Manually resolve conflicts and update rules; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm replication completing without conflicts; Step 6 - Prevention: Implement conflict detection alerting.
column5: Two source buckets replicating same prefix causing conflicts. Updated replication rules with non-overlapping prefixes. Conflicts resolved manually. Replication completing successfully. Conflict alerting implemented.
column12: Contact Storage Team for replication conflicts. Review replication architecture. Update replication configuration standards. Implement replication health monitoring.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge secret version limit warning; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check version count and cleanup policy; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify versions eligible for deletion; Step 4 - Action: Delete old versions and update cleanup policy; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm version count below limit; Step 6 - Automation: Implement automated version cleanup.
column5: Secret had 50 versions exceeding 20 version limit. Deleted versions older than 90 days. Implemented Lambda for automated cleanup. Version count now 15. Cleanup policy documented.
column12: Escalate to Security Team before deleting versions. Maintain minimum version history. Update secret lifecycle management. Implement version count alerting.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect query timeout and size limit errors; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check query complexity and result set size; Phase 3 - Investigation: Identify queries exceeding limits; Step 4 - Optimization: Add pagination and result limits; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm queries complete successfully; Step 6 - Documentation: Update query best practices.
column5: Dashboard query returning 1M+ results causing timeout. Added result limit of 10000 and pagination. Queries completing in <5 seconds. Best practices updated with query limits. Query optimization guide created.
column12: Escalate to Observability Team for query optimization. Review dashboard query efficiency. Update query performance standards. Implement query cost monitoring.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge duplicate data detection; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check transactional guarantees and idempotency; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify where duplicates introduced; Step 4 - Fix: Implement idempotent sinks and transactions; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm no duplicate processing; Step 6 - Testing: Add duplicate detection tests.
column5: Sink was not idempotent causing duplicates on retry. Implemented idempotent write with unique key. No duplicates in 48-hour test. Duplicate detection tests added. Processing guarantees documented.
column12: Escalate to Data Engineering for processing guarantee issues. Review exactly-once architecture. Update pipeline reliability standards. Implement data quality validation.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor configuration drift alerts; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check actual vs expected configuration; Phase 3 - Investigation: Identify drift detection logic issue; Step 4 - Fix: Update drift detection to ignore allowed differences; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm false positives eliminated; Step 6 - Tuning: Adjust drift detection sensitivity.
column5: Drift detection flagging dynamic config values as drift. Updated detection to exclude dynamic fields. False positives eliminated. Detection sensitivity tuned. Drift alert accuracy improved to 99%.
column12: Escalate to Platform Team if real drift missed. Review drift detection rules. Update configuration management guidelines. Implement drift trend analysis.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge tracing storage capacity warning; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check sampling rate and trace volume; Step 3 - Investigation: Analyze trace storage growth rate; Step 4 - Optimization: Reduce sampling rate for high-volume services; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm storage growth manageable; Step 6 - Documentation: Update sampling configuration.

column12: Escalate to Observability Team for sampling changes. Review trace retention requirements. Update observability cost management. Implement sampling rate monitoring.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor SSL handshake failures from edge; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check certificate chain completeness; Phase 3 - Investigation: Identify missing intermediate certificates; Step 4 - Fix: Upload complete certificate chain to CDN; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm SSL handshake succeeds; Step 6 - Testing: Test from multiple clients and browsers.

column12: Escalate to Security Team for certificate issues. Implement certificate chain validation. Update SSL deployment procedures. Add certificate health monitoring.

column4: Phase 1 - Detection: Monitor container orchestration logs for ImagePullBackOff errors; Phase 2 - Diagnosis: Check registry authentication credentials and network connectivity; Phase 3 - Investigation: Verify image tag exists and permissions are correct; Phase 4 - Remediation: Update image pull secrets or correct image tag; Phase 5 - Validation: Confirm pod starts successfully with new image; Phase 6 - Documentation: Record credential rotation in security log.
column5: Updated image pull secret with new registry token. Corrected image tag from latest to specific version. Pod started successfully within 2 minutes. Credential rotation documented in vault. Incident closed after verification.
column12: Escalate to Security Team if authentication fails repeatedly. Implement automated secret rotation. Update container deployment runbook. Verify registry availability status.

column4: Step 1 - Alert: Acknowledge checkpoint latency warning from stream processor; Step 2 - Diagnose: Check state backend write speeds and network I/O; Step 3 - Investigation: Identify large state snapshots causing delays; Step 4 - Optimization: Incremental checkpointing and adjust interval; Step 5 - Verification: Confirm checkpoint duration returns to baseline; Step 6 - Monitoring: Watch state size growth over 24 hours.

column12: Escalate to Data Engineering if latency exceeds 1 minute. Review state backend capacity. Update streaming job configuration standards. Implement checkpoint alerting thresholds.

column4: Phase 1 - Monitoring: Detect batch job queue depth exceeding threshold; Phase 2 - Analysis: Check worker node health and availability; Phase 3 - Investigation: Identify slow jobs blocking queue; Step 4 - Remediation: Scale worker nodes and kill stuck jobs; Step 5 - Validation: Confirm queue depth decreasing; Step 6 - Documentation: Update job timeout settings.
